front: Conditional expectation as Radon-Nikodym projection (4.1 Conditional Expectation)
back: E(X|F) is the F-measurable integrable random variable Y satisfying integral_A Y dP = integral_A X dP for every A in F. Exam use: The starting point for every martingale verification and every conditioning calculation. Pitfall: Conditional expectations are only unique up to almost sure equality, so pointwise formulas need version awareness.

front: Uniqueness and locality of conditional expectation (4.1 Conditional Expectation)
back: Versions of E(X|F) agree almost surely; if X1=X2 on B in F, then their conditional expectations agree on B almost surely. Exam use: Use locality to compute conditional expectations piecewise on atoms or events known at the conditioning time. Pitfall: The event where the variables agree must belong to the conditioning sigma-field.

front: Conditional expectation on a countable partition (4.1.1 Examples)
back: If F is generated by a countable positive-probability partition, E(X|F) is constant on each atom and equals the average of X over that atom. Exam use: Turns abstract conditioning into the familiar finite-state formula used in Bayes and Markov-chain problems. Pitfall: Atoms with probability zero cannot be handled by dividing by P(atom).

front: Conditional density formula (4.1.1 Examples)
back: For a joint density f(x,y), E(g(X)|Y)=h(Y), where h(y) is the conditional-density average of g against f(x,y). Exam use: Use for regression-style computations and conditional distribution questions with joint densities. Pitfall: Conditioning on Y=y is a density formula, not literal conditioning on a positive-probability event.

front: Conditioning with independent variables (4.1.1 Examples)
back: If X and Y are independent and g(x)=E phi(x,Y), then E(phi(X,Y)|X)=g(X). Exam use: A quick way to integrate out fresh independent noise while leaving the present state fixed. Pitfall: Do not replace Y by its mean unless phi is linear in Y.

front: Linearity, monotonicity, and monotone convergence conditionally (4.1.2 Properties)
back: Conditional expectation is linear, preserves order, and respects monotone limits under the same integrability hypotheses as ordinary expectation. Exam use: These are the algebra rules behind almost every martingale manipulation. Pitfall: Check integrability hypotheses before using linearity with signed variables.

front: Conditional Jensen and Lp contraction (4.1.2 Properties)
back: For convex phi, phi(E(X|F)) <= E(phi(X)|F); in particular conditional expectation is a contraction on Lp for p >= 1. Exam use: Use to prove submartingale transforms and uniform Lp bounds after conditioning. Pitfall: The inequality is almost sure, and the convex function must be integrable after composition.

front: Tower property: the smaller sigma-field wins (4.1.2 Properties)
back: If F1 subset F2, then E(E(X|F1)|F2)=E(X|F1) and E(E(X|F2)|F1)=E(X|F1). Exam use: Essential for collapsing repeated conditionings in martingale and Markov arguments. Pitfall: The order of unrelated sigma-fields cannot be swapped; nesting is the whole point.

front: Taking out what is known (4.1.2 Properties)
back: If X is F-measurable and the products are integrable, then E(XY|F)=X E(Y|F). Exam use: Lets predictable factors pass through conditional expectation in martingale increment calculations. Pitfall: The multiplier must be measurable with respect to the conditioning sigma-field.

front: Conditional expectation as L2 best predictor (4.1.2 Properties)
back: When EX^2 is finite, E(X|F) is the F-measurable square-integrable variable minimizing E(X-Y)^2. Exam use: Useful for variance decompositions and interpreting conditional expectation as optimal prediction. Pitfall: The projection picture needs second moments; L1 conditional expectation exists more generally.

front: Regular conditional distributions (4.1.3 Regular Conditional Probabilities*)
back: A regular conditional distribution gives a probability kernel whose A-coordinate versions equal P(X in A|G). Exam use: Use when a problem wants conditioning as a genuine conditional law rather than one function at a time. Pitfall: Regular conditional probabilities need not exist on arbitrary measurable spaces.

front: Martingale, submartingale, and supermartingale definitions (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: An adapted integrable process is a martingale when E(X_{n+1}|F_n)=X_n; replacing equality by >= or <= gives submartingales or supermartingales. Exam use: Every martingale problem starts by identifying the filtration, integrability, adaptedness, and the one-step conditional expectation. Pitfall: The filtration is part of the data; a process can be a martingale for one filtration and not another.

front: Linear, quadratic, and exponential random-walk martingales (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For iid increments, S_n-n mu, S_n^2-n sigma^2 in the centered finite-variance case, and exp(theta S_n)/phi(theta)^n are martingales under their natural hypotheses. Exam use: These are the main engines for gambler's ruin, hitting probabilities, Wald identities, and exponential tail bounds. Pitfall: The exponential martingale requires the moment generating function to be finite at the chosen theta.

front: Multi-step conditional expectation property (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For n>m, martingales satisfy E(X_n|F_m)=X_m; submartingales give >= and supermartingales give <=. Exam use: Use whenever a future stopped or terminal value is related back to present information. Pitfall: The direction of the inequality reverses between submartingales and supermartingales.

front: Convex transforms make submartingales (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: A convex function of a martingale is a submartingale; an increasing convex function of a submartingale is also a submartingale. Exam use: Build nonnegative submartingales such as |X_n|, X_n^2, and exp(theta X_n) for maximal inequalities. Pitfall: The transformed variables must be integrable.

front: Martingale transforms and no profitable gambling systems (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If H_n is nonnegative predictable and X is a supermartingale, then the gains process sum H_m(X_m-X_{m-1}) is a supermartingale. Exam use: Formalizes optional betting strategies and proves stopped supermartingales remain supermartingales. Pitfall: Predictability is essential; stakes cannot depend on the next outcome.

front: Stopped processes preserve martingale type (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If N is a stopping time and X is a supermartingale, then X_{N wedge n} is a supermartingale; analogous statements hold for martingales and submartingales. Exam use: Use before applying convergence or inequality theorems to hitting-time stopped processes. Pitfall: Stopping alone does not guarantee equality of expectations at unbounded times.

front: Doob upcrossing inequality (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For a submartingale, the expected number of upcrossings of [a,b] up to n is bounded by E(X_n-a)^+/(b-a). Exam use: The key tool for proving almost sure convergence from bounded positive expectations. Pitfall: The interval is [a,b] with a<b; upcrossings are completed crossings, not mere visits.

front: Submartingale almost sure convergence theorem (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If X_n is a submartingale with sup_n E X_n^+ finite, then X_n converges almost surely to a finite limit. Exam use: A default convergence theorem for bounded or nonnegative martingales after choosing the right sign. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence does not automatically imply L1 convergence or preservation of expectations.

front: Nonnegative supermartingale convergence (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: Every nonnegative supermartingale converges almost surely to a finite limit, with finite expected limit bounded by the initial expectation. Exam use: Extremely useful for likelihood ratios, branching process normalizations, and stopped random walks. Pitfall: The limit can have smaller expectation than X_0; equality needs uniform integrability or stronger optional stopping conditions.

front: Bounded-increment convergence criterion (4.3.1 Bounded Increments)
back: A supermartingale with bounded increments cannot keep drifting on a bounded interval without converging; stopping and shifting make it nonnegative. Exam use: Use for random walks or games where increments are controlled but the process is not globally nonnegative. Pitfall: You must first localize so the shifted stopped process is nonnegative.

front: Doob decomposition (4.3 Examples)
back: Every submartingale decomposes uniquely into a martingale plus an increasing predictable process starting at zero. Exam use: Separates random fluctuation from predictable drift in counting-process and indicator-sum arguments. Pitfall: The compensator is predictable, not optional; its increment is known one step earlier.

front: Conditional Borel-Cantelli lemma (4.3 Examples)
back: For adapted events B_n, occurrence infinitely often is controlled by the divergence or convergence of the conditional probability sums E(1_{B_n}|F_{n-1}). Exam use: Use for dependent trials where ordinary independence is unavailable but conditional probabilities are tractable. Pitfall: The conditioning must be on the past; replacing it by unconditional probabilities loses the theorem's content.

front: Polya urn proportion martingale (4.3.2 Polya's Urn Scheme)
back: In Polya's urn, the fraction of balls of one color is a bounded martingale and therefore converges almost surely. Exam use: A model example for bounded martingale convergence and beta-limit intuition. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence alone does not identify the limiting distribution without extra computation.

front: Radon-Nikodym derivative martingales (4.3.3 Radon-Nikodym Derivatives)
back: If mu_n and nu_n are restrictions to F_n with mu_n absolutely continuous with respect to nu_n, then dmu_n/dnu_n forms a nonnegative martingale under nu. Exam use: Use to compare infinite product measures and prove absolute-continuity versus singularity alternatives. Pitfall: Finite-dimensional absolute continuity does not by itself guarantee infinite-dimensional absolute continuity.

front: Branching process normalized martingale (4.3.4 Branching Processes)
back: For a Galton-Watson process with mean mu, Z_n/mu^n is a nonnegative martingale when mu is positive. Exam use: The central martingale for extinction, survival, and supercritical growth questions. Pitfall: In the supercritical case the martingale limit may still be zero unless an L log L condition holds.

front: Bounded optional sampling inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: If X is a submartingale and N is a bounded stopping time, then E X_0 <= E X_N <= E X_k for a bound k on N. Exam use: The bounded-time version of optional sampling used inside maximal inequality proofs. Pitfall: Boundedness of the stopping time is doing real work; unbounded stopping can fail.

front: Doob maximal inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For a submartingale, lambda P(max_{m<=n} X_m >= lambda) is bounded by the expected terminal value on the event that the maximum exceeds lambda. Exam use: Use to control suprema of martingales, derive Kolmogorov inequalities, and prove Lp convergence. Pitfall: Apply it to nonnegative submartingales such as |X_n| or X_n^2 when signs are an issue.

front: Doob Lp maximum inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For p>1, the Lp norm of max_{m<=n} |X_m| is bounded by p/(p-1) times the Lp norm of X_n for martingales and suitable submartingales. Exam use: The main route from uniform Lp bounds to almost sure and Lp convergence. Pitfall: There is no comparable L1 maximal inequality without extra hypotheses.

front: Lp martingale convergence theorem (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: If a martingale has sup_n E|X_n|^p finite for some p>1, then X_n converges almost surely and in Lp. Exam use: A clean sufficient condition for exchanging martingale limits with p-th moments. Pitfall: The theorem needs p>1; L1 convergence requires uniform integrability instead.

front: Orthogonality of square-integrable martingale increments (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: Square-integrable martingale increments are orthogonal, so variances of sums add across increments. Exam use: Use for L2 bounds, variance computations, and quadratic martingale arguments. Pitfall: Orthogonality is not independence; it is a second-moment identity.

front: Conditional variance formula for martingales (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For square-integrable martingales, conditional second-moment growth is the sum of conditional variances of increments. Exam use: Use to estimate quadratic variation and prove convergence from summable conditional variances. Pitfall: Use conditional variances of increments, not unconditional variances unless independence or identical structure justifies it.

front: Square-integrable martingales and quadratic variation (4.5 Square Integrable Martingales*)
back: For square-integrable martingales, the predictable accumulated conditional variance A_n governs maximal L2 bounds and convergence. Exam use: Use when convergence depends on summability of conditional variances rather than bounded increments. Pitfall: The predictable quadratic variation is not the same as the realized squared-increment sum.

front: Martingale strong-law normalization (4.5 Square Integrable Martingales*)
back: If a square-integrable martingale has controlled predictable quadratic variation, then X_n normalized by a suitable increasing function of A_n tends to zero. Exam use: Generalizes Kolmogorov strong-law arguments to martingale differences. Pitfall: Choose a normalization satisfying the required integral/summability condition.

front: Uniform integrability (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: A family is uniformly integrable when the tail expectations sup E(|X|; |X|>K) go to zero as K goes to infinity. Exam use: The exact condition needed to upgrade probability or a.s. convergence to L1 convergence. Pitfall: Bounded L1 norms alone do not imply uniform integrability.

front: Uniform integrability plus probability convergence gives L1 convergence (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If X_n is uniformly integrable and X_n converges in probability to X, then X is integrable and X_n converges to X in L1. Exam use: Use after proving almost sure martingale convergence to obtain L1 convergence. Pitfall: Convergence in probability without uniform integrability is not enough for L1 convergence.

front: Submartingale L1 convergence equivalences (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: For submartingales, uniform integrability is equivalent to almost sure and L1 convergence to an integrable limit under the standard convergence hypotheses. Exam use: Decides when the limit preserves first moments. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence from Chapter 4.2 by itself is weaker than L1 convergence.

front: Closed martingales and uniform integrability (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: A martingale is uniformly integrable iff it converges in L1 iff it can be written as X_n=E(X|F_n) for some integrable terminal variable X. Exam use: The main structural test for optional stopping at unbounded times. Pitfall: A martingale can converge a.s. but fail to be closed by an L1 terminal variable.

front: Levy upward theorem (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If F_n increases to F_infinity, then E(X|F_n) converges almost surely and in L1 to E(X|F_infinity). Exam use: Use to prove Levy's 0-1 law and conditional dominated convergence. Pitfall: The limiting sigma-field is generated by the increasing union, not simply the pointwise union as a collection.

front: Levy's 0-1 law (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If A belongs to the limiting sigma-field of an increasing filtration, then P(A|F_n) converges almost surely to 1_A. Exam use: Use to convert better and better finite information into eventual certainty for tail-like events inside the generated limit. Pitfall: This is different from Kolmogorov's 0-1 law, which concerns independent tail sigma-fields.

front: Backward martingale convergence (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: A martingale indexed backward in time, with decreasing sigma-fields, converges almost surely and in L1 to its conditional expectation on the tail intersection. Exam use: The engine behind the strong law, Hewitt-Savage, and de Finetti-style results. Pitfall: The index direction matters: sigma-fields decrease as time moves backward.

front: Levy downward theorem (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: If F_n decreases to F_{-infinity}, then E(Y|F_n) converges almost surely and in L1 to E(Y|F_{-infinity}). Exam use: Use for exchangeability, tail sigma-fields, and reverse filtrations. Pitfall: Do not replace the intersection by a limiting union; this is the downward theorem.

front: Strong law via backward martingales (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: For iid integrable variables, sample averages can be represented through decreasing symmetric-information sigma-fields and converge to the mean. Exam use: A conceptual proof of the strong law that also prepares de Finetti arguments. Pitfall: The iid and integrability assumptions are doing the work; this is not a variance proof.

front: Hewitt-Savage 0-1 law (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: For iid sequences, every event invariant under finite permutations has probability 0 or 1. Exam use: Use when an event is exchangeable rather than merely tail-measurable. Pitfall: Permutation invariance is stronger/different than tail measurability; check the event carefully.

front: de Finetti theorem for exchangeable sequences (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: Exchangeable sequences are conditionally iid given the exchangeable tail information; in the binary case they are mixtures of iid Bernoulli sequences. Exam use: Recognize hidden-random-parameter representations in exchangeable models. Pitfall: Exchangeability is weaker than independence but stronger than identical marginal distributions.

front: Optional stopping for uniformly integrable martingales (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: Uniformly integrable martingales satisfy optional sampling for arbitrary stopping times, with stopped values inheriting the martingale inequalities. Exam use: The cleanest condition under which E X_N = E X_0 is valid for unbounded N. Pitfall: Without uniform integrability or another sufficient condition, optional stopping can fail dramatically.

front: Optional stopping with integrable terminal value (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: If X_N is integrable and the pre-stopping tails X_n 1_{N>n} are uniformly integrable, then X_{N wedge n} is uniformly integrable and optional stopping applies. Exam use: A practical checklist for hitting times where the stopped value is bounded or controlled. Pitfall: Finite expectation of N alone is not the same as this UI tail condition, though bounded increments can imply useful variants.

front: Wald's equation via optional stopping (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: For iid integrable increments with mean mu, S_N has expectation mu E N under suitable stopping and integrability conditions. Exam use: Use for expected sums at stopping times, especially random walks and renewal-style counts. Pitfall: Independence of N from increments is not the right general condition; N is often a stopping time, and optional stopping hypotheses must be checked.

front: Symmetric gambler's ruin via martingales (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: For symmetric simple random walk stopped on hitting a or b, optional stopping of S_n gives linear hitting probabilities and S_n^2-n gives expected hitting times. Exam use: High-yield prelim template for hitting probabilities and mean exit times. Pitfall: For one-sided unbounded hitting times, use truncation or extra arguments before sending the bound to infinity.

front: Asymmetric gambler's ruin via exponential martingales (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: For biased simple random walk, an exponential martingale r^{S_n} gives nonlinear hitting probabilities and finite expected hitting times in the drift direction. Exam use: Use when the walk has drift and the harmonic function is exponential rather than linear. Pitfall: The symmetric formulas are limiting cases; plugging p=q into biased formulas may require taking a limit.

front: Insurance ruin exponential bound (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: If a random walk has a positive adjustment coefficient theta with E exp(theta xi)=1, exponential martingales bound the probability of ever hitting a bad lower level. Exam use: Use for Cramer-Lundberg style ruin bounds and rare-event hitting estimates. Pitfall: The bound needs the exponential moment equation at a positive theta.

front: Reflection principle (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: For simple random walk paths, paths crossing a barrier can be counted by reflecting the path after the first hit. Exam use: Use for exact hitting and maximum distributions without martingale machinery. Pitfall: The reflection is a path-counting bijection; keep parity and endpoints consistent.

front: Ballot theorem (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: If one candidate finishes ahead, the probability that they stayed strictly ahead throughout has a simple ratio depending on the final margin. Exam use: Use for positivity-conditioned walks and first-passage combinatorics. Pitfall: Strict positivity versus nonnegativity changes the boundary and constants.

front: Arcsine laws for simple random walk (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: The last zero time and the occupation time above zero for long symmetric simple random walks have arcsine limiting distributions. Exam use: Recognize that typical paths spend a surprisingly large fraction of time mostly positive or mostly negative. Pitfall: The arcsine density is U-shaped; intuition based on concentration near one half is wrong.
